sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
Chest pain
History of Present Illness
Elizabeth is a 53-year-old female complaining of chest pain. She has a past medical history of diabetes and high blood pressure. She reports the pain occurs when walking and describes it as a dull pressure. It has been present for approximately three months. She notes that it is exacerbated by walking and relieved by rest. She has been trying to avoid walking due to the pain. Her blood sugars have been higher recently due to reduced activity. She is currently taking metformin 500mg twice daily. She has an ophthalmology appointment scheduled in two weeks. She has no nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. She has no known family history of heart disease. She was previously diagnosed with high blood pressure by another clinician, but she is not currently on any medication for it. She is aware of this history but does not recall being actively managed for it.
Findings
Physical exam reveals a two out of six systolic ejection murmur. The lungs are clear to auscultation. The heart rhythm is regular at 72 beats per minute. The extremities are warm and dry. There is one plus edema in the ankles bilaterally. The abdomen is soft and non-tender. The back is unremarkable. An EKG shows no acute ischemic changes.
Assessment

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
The patient's presentation of chest pain raises concern for possible unstable angina, given her history of diabetes and hypertension. Further investigation is warranted to rule out coronary artery disease.
Plan
I am ordering a stat aspirin 81 mg daily. I am also prescribing nitroglycerin for sublingual use if chest pain occurs. I will order a hemoglobin A1c to assess her diabetic control. I will refer her to cardiology for a stress test to evaluate her cardiac function. I will also start her on hydrochlorothiazide 25mg daily to help manage her blood pressure and edema. Please monitor your blood pressure at home and report any findings to Epic MyChart. Please follow up in two weeks to assess progress.
